stimulus:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

  • American writers Ralph Waldo Emerson and Henry David Thoreau frequently exchanged journals and essays in the 1840s.
  • Some essays now attributed to one writer may have included substantial passages by the other.
  • The precise authorship of certain paragraphs in essays such as Thoreau's "Walking" is disputed.
  • Computational linguists Patel, Owusu, and Reyes used statistical stylometry to analyze word-frequency patterns in the contested texts.
  • They concluded that the opening of "Walking" was most likely written by Thoreau alone.
stem: The student wants to make a generalization about the kind of study conducted by Patel, Owusu, and Reyes. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

answerOptions.content: Drawing on statistical stylometry, Patel, Owusu, and Reyes claim that Thoreau wrote the opening of "Walking."
It is unlikely that Emerson wrote the opening of "Walking"; Thoreau is the more probable author.
It is likely that Henry David Thoreau, not Ralph Waldo Emerson, wrote the opening of "Walking."
Researchers have used statistical methods to investigate questions of authorship within nineteenth-century American literature.

rationale:
Choice D is the best answer because it generalizes about the study's broader category—statistical authorship attribution within nineteenth-century American literature—rather than reporting a single finding.

Choices A, B, and C all report specific conclusions about "Walking," which is too narrow to count as a generalization about the kind of study.
